criteria.fact: This paper offers a comprehensive benchmark study of centralized and federated ML algorithms for heart disease classification using the UCI heart disease dataset [1].

criteria.criterion: <fact>It combines FedAvg’s model aggregation with ADAM’s adaptive learning rate mechanism, allowing FedAdam to handle non-stationary and sparse gradients typically encountered in FL settings.</fact> <scope>from paper context</scope>
criteria.fact: It combines FedAvg’s model aggregation with ADAM’s adaptive learning rate mechanism, allowing FedAdam to handle non-stationary and sparse gradients typically encountered in FL settings.

criteria.criterion: <fact>Client updates are computed as: $$ \begin{array}{l} \Delta_ {t} = \frac {1}{| S |} \sum_ {i \in S} \Delta_ {i} ^ {t} \\ m _ {t} = \beta_ {1} m _ {t - 1} + (1 - \beta_ {1}) \Delta_ {t} \tag {1} \\ v _ {t} = \beta_ {2} v _ {t - 1} + (1 - \beta_ {2}) (\Delta_ {t}) ^ {2} \\ x _ {t + 1} = x _ {t} - \eta \frac {m _ {t}}{\sqrt {v _ {t}} + \tau} \\ \end{array} $$ where $\Delta _ { t }$ is the aggregated gradient update at time t, $S$ is the set of clients participating, $| S |$ is the number of clients in $S$ , $m _ { t }$ and $v _ { t }$ are the first and second moment estimates, $\beta _ { 1 }$ and $\beta _ { 2 }$ are the exponential decay rates for these moment estimates, $\eta$ is the learning rate, and $\tau$ is a small constant for numerical stability.</fact> <scope>from paper context</scope>
criteria.fact: Client updates are computed as: $$ \begin{array}{l} \Delta_ {t} = \frac {1}{| S |} \sum_ {i \in S} \Delta_ {i} ^ {t} \\ m _ {t} = \beta_ {1} m _ {t - 1} + (1 - \beta_ {1}) \Delta_ {t} \tag {1} \\ v _ {t} = \beta_ {2} v _ {t - 1} + (1 - \beta_ {2}) (\Delta_ {t}) ^ {2} \\ x _ {t + 1} = x _ {t} - \eta \frac {m _ {t}}{\sqrt {v _ {t}} + \tau} \\ \end{array} $$ where $\Delta _ { t }$ is the aggregated gradient update at time t, $S$ is the set of clients participating, $| S |$ is the number of clients in $S$ , $m _ { t }$ and $v _ { t }$ are the first and second moment estimates, $\beta _ { 1 }$ and $\beta _ { 2 }$ are the exponential decay rates for these moment estimates, $\eta$ is the learning rate, and $\tau$ is a small constant for numerical stability.

criteria.criterion: <fact>Specifically, to address the issue of heterogeneity, FedYogi dynamically adjusts the learning rate based on the gradients’ first and second moments, $m _ { t }$ and $v _ { t }$ , respectively.</fact> <scope>from paper context</scope>
criteria.fact: Specifically, to address the issue of heterogeneity, FedYogi dynamically adjusts the learning rate based on the gradients’ first and second moments, $m _ { t }$ and $v _ { t }$ , respectively.

criteria.criterion: <fact>Updates for the global and local control variables and model parameters are computed as follows: $$ \begin{array}{l} \boldsymbol {y} _ {i} \leftarrow \boldsymbol {y} _ {i} - \eta_ {l} \left(g _ {i} \left(\boldsymbol {y} _ {i}\right) + \boldsymbol {c} - \boldsymbol {c} _ {i}\right) \\ c _ {i} ^ {+} \leftarrow (\mathrm {i}) g _ {i} (\boldsymbol {x}), \text {o r (i i)} \boldsymbol {c} _ {i} - \boldsymbol {c} + \frac {1}{K \eta_ {l}} (\boldsymbol {x} - \boldsymbol {y} _ {i}) \\ \boldsymbol {x} \leftarrow \boldsymbol {x} + \frac {\eta_ {g}}{| S |} \sum_ {i \in S} (\boldsymbol {y} _ {i} - \boldsymbol {x}) \\ \boldsymbol {c} \gets \boldsymbol {c} + \frac {1}{N} \sum_ {i \in S} (\boldsymbol {c} _ {\boldsymbol {i}} ^ {+} - \boldsymbol {c} _ {\boldsymbol {i}}) \\ \end{array} $$ where $\mathbf { { \mathit { y } } } _ { i }$ is client update performed $K$ times, $\eta$ is the learning rate, $g _ { i } ( y _ { i } )$ is the mini-batch gradient, and $_ { \textbf { \em x } }$ are the server parameters.</fact> <scope>from paper context</scope>
criteria.fact: Updates for the global and local control variables and model parameters are computed as follows: $$ \begin{array}{l} \boldsymbol {y} _ {i} \leftarrow \boldsymbol {y} _ {i} - \eta_ {l} \left(g _ {i} \left(\boldsymbol {y} _ {i}\right) + \boldsymbol {c} - \boldsymbol {c} _ {i}\right) \\ c _ {i} ^ {+} \leftarrow (\mathrm {i}) g _ {i} (\boldsymbol {x}), \text {o r (i i)} \boldsymbol {c} _ {i} - \boldsymbol {c} + \frac {1}{K \eta_ {l}} (\boldsymbol {x} - \boldsymbol {y} _ {i}) \\ \boldsymbol {x} \leftarrow \boldsymbol {x} + \frac {\eta_ {g}}{| S |} \sum_ {i \in S} (\boldsymbol {y} _ {i} - \boldsymbol {x}) \\ \boldsymbol {c} \gets \boldsymbol {c} + \frac {1}{N} \sum_ {i \in S} (\boldsymbol {c} _ {\boldsymbol {i}} ^ {+} - \boldsymbol {c} _ {\boldsymbol {i}}) \\ \end{array} $$ where $\mathbf { { \mathit { y } } } _ { i }$ is client update performed $K$ times, $\eta$ is the learning rate, $g _ { i } ( y _ { i } )$ is the mini-batch gradient, and $_ { \textbf { \em x } }$ are the server parameters.

criteria.criterion: <fact>Results The results for the pooled data are presented in Table II, which highlights the testing accuracies for adopted models.</fact> <scope>from paper context</scope>
criteria.fact: Results The results for the pooled data are presented in Table II, which highlights the testing accuracies for adopted models.

criteria.criterion: <fact>Logistic regression (LR), one-layer neural network (1LNN), support vector machine (SVM) with a linear kernel achieve the highest accuracies, with SVM leading at $8 3 .</fact> <scope>from paper context</scope>
criteria.fact: Logistic regression (LR), one-layer neural network (1LNN), support vector machine (SVM) with a linear kernel achieve the highest accuracies, with SVM leading at $8 3 .

criteria.criterion: <fact>We compare test accuracies (averaged over 10 seeds) of LR, 1LNN, and SVM local models and using FedAvg, FedAdam, FedYogi and SCAFFOLD FL algorithms.</fact> <scope>from paper context</scope>
